metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The best analogy for the program is a working group, and it is deliberately not for everyone. Fellows must show their reasoning and defend their decisions, not just produce results. There is no guaranteed timeline; progress depends on real work rather than a fixed schedule. It operates as a nonprofit functioning like an open-source collective crossed with a compressed PhD program.

artifactLines: The best analogy for the program is a working group, and it is deliberately not for everyone.
Fellows must show their reasoning and defend their decisions, not just produce results.
There is no guaranteed timeline; progress depends on real work rather than a fixed schedule.
It operates as a nonprofit functioning like an open-source collective crossed with a compressed PhD program.

beats.narration_text: The framing that this runs like an open-source collective crossed with a compressed PhD, with no guaranteed timeline, made me ask if I could actually survive it. I want to self-assess honestly. Help me: (1) pick one real project I could ship and define what 'shipping' would concretely mean; (2) rehearse defending my key decisions out loud as if challenged; (3) design a weekly cadence that mimics a working group so I build the reasoning muscle, not just the output.
